stage.name: Blockchain Foundations
stage.description: SHA-256, Merkle trees, Proof-of-Work vs Proof-of-Stake, Byzantine fault tolerance, and the blockchain trilemma.

elements.content:
Structure

Each block contains: block header (version, prev_hash, merkle_root, timestamp, nBits, nonce), transaction list, and coinbase tx

Blocks are linked via SHA-256 hash of previous header — altering any block breaks every subsequent hash

Genesis block (block 0) is hardcoded — no previous hash

Immutability

To rewrite block N you must redo proof-of-work for N, N+1, N+2… and outpace the rest of the network

Bitcoin produces ~1 block per 10 min; 6 confirmations = ~1 hour of work an attacker must redo

Cost to 51%-attack Bitcoin: ~$20B+ in ASICs + $15M/hour in electricity

Decentralization

~15,000 full nodes independently verify every block and transaction

No node is trusted — each verifies the full chain from genesis

Consensus = longest valid chain with most accumulated proof-of-work

5 Formal Properties

Pre-image resistance: given H(x), finding x requires 2²⁵⁶ operations

Second pre-image: given x, finding x′ ≠ x with H(x′)=H(x) is infeasible

Collision resistance: finding any (x,x′) pair with same hash is infeasible

Avalanche effect: flipping 1 bit changes ~50% of output bits

Deterministic: same input always produces identical output

SHA-256 in Bitcoin

Block header hashed TWICE — SHA-256(SHA-256(header)) prevents length-extension attacks

Mining = find nonce such that hash < target (a 256-bit number)

Difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks) to maintain 10-min block time

Current difficulty: ~88 trillion — one in 88T nonces is valid

Why It Secures Bitcoin

Changing any tx changes Merkle root → changes block hash

Changed block hash → breaks linkage to next block

Attacker must redo all proof-of-work for the entire chain — computationally infeasible

SHA-256 has zero known cryptographic breaks after 20+ years of scrutiny

Structure

Binary hash tree: leaf nodes = H(Tx), internal nodes = H(left_child ∥ right_child)

Root = Merkle Root — embedded in block header (32 bytes)

4,000-tx block: tree depth = 12, proof = 12 × 32 = 384 bytes vs 4,000 × 250 bytes full block

Changing any single tx changes every hash up to the root — instant fraud detection

SPV Clients

Simple Payment Verification nodes download only 80-byte block headers (~70 MB total)

To verify a payment, request the Merkle proof branch (12 hashes = 384 bytes)

Compare computed root against block header root — no need for full 350 MB block

Used by mobile wallets — enables Bitcoin on low-resource devices

Patricia Merkle Tries

Ethereum extends Merkle trees to Modified Patricia Tries (MPT)

Stores state (accounts), transactions, and receipts in separate tries

Each try root is in the block header — enables O(log n) state proofs

Critical for stateless clients and Ethereum's verkle tree migration

Proof-of-Work

Miners compete to find nonce: SHA-256²(header) < target

Difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks to maintain 10-min average

51% attack cost: must control majority of global hash rate

Energy use is a feature — makes past blocks expensive to rewrite

Nakamoto consensus: follow the chain with most accumulated work

Proof-of-Stake

Validators lock (stake) ETH as collateral — 32 ETH minimum to solo validate

Attestation committees randomly selected per epoch (6.4 min, 32 slots)

RANDAO + VDF provides verifiable randomness for validator selection

Slashing: equivocation or surround voting destroys up to 100% of stake

Finality via Casper FFG — 2 justified checkpoints = finalized, never reverted

Tradeoffs

PoW: battle-tested 15+ years, ASIC hardware creates real-world cost barrier

PoS: 99.95% less energy, faster finality, but 'nothing-at-stake' problem mitigated by slashing

PoW favors large mining operations; PoS favors large capital holders

Ethereum's Merge (2022) switched from PoW→PoS, reduced issuance by ~90%
Reaching consensus when some participants are malicious

The Problem

Byzantine Generals Problem (Lamport 1982): generals must coordinate attack but some may be traitors

A system is BFT if it reaches correct consensus despite f arbitrary (Byzantine) faults

Classic result: requires 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine faults

Bitcoin's PoW achieves probabilistic BFT without knowing who is Byzantine

PBFT Algorithm

Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (Castro & Liskov 1999) — first efficient BFT protocol

Three phases: pre-prepare → prepare → commit — requires 2 round-trips of O(n²) messages

Handles f < n/3 Byzantine nodes with deterministic safety and liveness

Used in Hyperledger Fabric, Tendermint, and many permissioned blockchains

Modern BFT Chains

Tendermint: PBFT-inspired, used in Cosmos — instant finality, ~1s block times

Ethereum's Casper: hybrid PoS+BFT — uses 2/3 supermajority attestations for finality

Solana's Tower BFT: optimistic confirmation in ~400ms, hard finality in ~12s

HotStuff (used by Diem/Aptos): linear message complexity O(n) vs PBFT's O(n²)

The Trilemma

Coined by Vitalik Buterin: blockchains can optimize for at most 2 of 3 properties

Security: resistance to attacks (51%, Sybil, eclipse)

Decentralization: no single entity controls consensus or state

Scalability: high throughput (TPS), low latency, low fees

Bitcoin maximizes security + decentralization; sacrifices scalability (7 TPS)

Layer-2 Solutions

Payment channels (Lightning): off-chain state, only settlement on-chain — Bitcoin achieves millions of TPS

Optimistic rollups (Arbitrum, Optimism): batch 100s of txs into one L1 tx, 7-day fraud proof window

ZK-rollups (StarkNet, zkSync): validity proof posted to L1 — instant finality, no fraud window

State channels + rollups = security from L1 + scalability from L2

Alternative Approaches

Sharding: split state into N shards, each processing its own txs in parallel (Ethereum's future)

DAG-based (IOTA, Nano): no linear chain — parallel tx confirmation, no miners

Avalanche consensus: probabilistic sampling — 4,500 TPS with sub-second finality

Tradeoff: all alternatives sacrifice some decentralization or security assumption

Smart Contract Basics

Code deployed to a blockchain address — immutable once deployed (unless upgradeable proxy)

Executed by every full node — output is deterministic (same input = same output on every node)

Gas system: every opcode costs gas, prevents infinite loops (halting problem workaround)

Solidity compiles to EVM bytecode — 256-bit stack, 32-byte word size

EVM Architecture

Stack-based VM: max 1024 depth, each element 32 bytes

Storage: persistent key-value store (expensive — 20,000 gas per SSTORE)

Memory: temporary byte array (cheap, linear cost)

CALL opcode enables contract-to-contract calls — foundation of DeFi composability

Precompiles: SHA-256, ECRECOVER, BN128 pairing — native implementations at fixed gas costs

Beyond EVM

EVM equivalence: Polygon zkEVM, Scroll produce ZK proofs of EVM execution

SVM (Solana): parallel execution via Sealevel — tracks which accounts a tx reads/writes

MoveVM (Aptos/Sui): resource-oriented types — assets can't be copied or dropped, only moved

WebAssembly (WASM): Polkadot, NEAR, CosmWasm — language-agnostic, near-native speed
